Content: @bunnie I am wiring up the CH347 to the NeTV2, will make a wiring diagram as part of making it work with Raspberry Pi 5, does the netv2 have an on board JTAG chip / programming port ? On the JTAG port attached to the Raspberry Pi is the RST pin equivalent of SRST on JTAG ?

Author: bunnievorpal
Content: the ⁨`RES`⁩ pin next to GND goes to PROGRAM_B on the FPGA

Author: bunnievorpal
Content: in other words, holding that low will fully reset the FPGA and cause it to reload its bitstream

Author: bunnievorpal
Content: it's not a jtag reset

Author: bunnievorpal
Content: it's a full system reset

Author: carlfk
Content: What is BMC serial?

Author: mithro_
Content: BMC == Board Management Controller -- it's a little Linux computer that is found on server motherboards which allows you to remotely access things like the power buttons, virtual vga screen, virtual serial port, etc....

Author: carlfk
Content: ok - I undersatnd BMC - how does serial fit in?

Author: mithro_
Content:
The BMC lets you connect to the computer's serial port so you can see things like very early BIOS messages or if the linux running on the system is configured correctly even a console. Provides a way to get access to the machine when SSH isn't working or similar. I wanted computers like tweed to output dmesg messages to their serial port and log them so I could see things like kernal panics and such, hence why I was looking at conserver.

But it seems like conserver could be useful for just generally providing remote access to usb serial ports in an RPi as it deals with the problem of multiple devices trying to access the serial port at once (IE you could be connected with something like cu at the same time you connect little_term and another python script)....

Author: tishamdhar
Content:
Install LiteX following the standard setup, then:

cd litex-boards/litex_boards/targets

# Build for XC7A35T variant with Ethernet
./kosagi_netv2.py --variant=a7-35 --with-ethernet --build

# Or for the 100T variant with PCIe
./kosagi_netv2.py --variant=a7-100 --with-pcie --build
The target supports:

DDR3 SDRAM: Using the K4B2G1646F module with LiteDRAM
Ethernet: RMII PHY via LiteEth
PCIe: x4 Gen2 via LitePCIe
SD Card: Both SPI mode and native SDIO
LED Chaser: For the obligatory blinky demo

@RebelMike - I have a crazy idea of having the RPi5 emulate larger (IE megabyte sized) spiflash/psram with the PIO for use both with Tiny Tapeout and BMC motherboard development -- kinda of a more advanced version of your emulation on the RP2040.

Author: 246tnt
Content: I'm not sure what kind of latency you'd manage to get given there is PCIe in the middle ...

Author: rebelmike
Content: I suspect getting deterministic performance over the PCIe bus would be tricky. I would have thought a RP2350 with PSRAM attached (maybe to GPIO instead of through XIP) would be preferable

Author: rebelmike
Content: Although at that point why not just actually attach the PSRAM to the project. I guess you could support slightly different protocols, although never at a high clock speed

Author: rebelmike
Content: One thing I think Pi 5 could be great at that I haven’t seen (though I haven’t looked recently) is a cheap logic analyser. PIO should be able to sample pins at 200MHz and you have reasonably fast access to GBs of memory

Content: For USB there's https://github.com/sekigon-gonnoc/Pico-PIO-USB which only needs one PIO so might work - probably mainly depends on whether there's any latency issues.
